GRU Space is now hiring a Founding Avionics Engineer. Apply here: https://lnkd.in/gFaupNka What you’ll do: - Design the avionics stack: controller boards, power distribution, sensor interfaces and valve actuation electronics. - Write the flight software for payload sequencing, closed-loop process control, and fault management. - Own the electrical interface to the CLPS lander (power, data, commanding, telemetry formatting). - Select and qualify components for the lunar radiation and thermal environment. - Design and build harnessing, including a lunar umbilical cord exposed on the Moon for our deployable payload. What we’re looking for: - Experience designing mixed-signal PCBs from schematic through layout, fabrication, and bring-up (full-cycle). RS-485 / serial protocol design: you can write a protocol ICD and define baud rate, framing, CRC, and fault handling from scratch. - Flight software in C/C++ on microcontrollers: state machines, FDIR, watchdog logic, closed-loop control. - Familiarity with spaceflight-adjacent standards for wiring, grounding, and EMI/EMC. - Power electronics: 28 Vdc conditioning, inrush limiting, motor driver integration, and power budget analysis. - Harness design: MIL-STD-38999 connectors, EMI/EMC compliance, thermal-rated wire and insulation (Kapton/PTFE), shielding continuity. - Comfort operating across the hardware/software boundary: you can debug a board with an oscilloscope and a JTAG probe. - You’ve built flight hardware before - Excellent communication skills

Last week, our latest founding mechanical engineer quit SpaceX pre-IPO pre-full vest, and drove up all the way from Texas to SF to join GRU. Now, we have one final spot left in our Founding Team: Founding Avionics Engineer. You’ll design the electronics and flight software that bring our 2027 lunar payload to life: commanding the ISRU brick fabrication process, inflating a habitat bladder, and bringing the data back to Earth. You will join us in changing the course of human history when we inevitably make the world's first bricks on the Moon and usher in a new era of industrialization on the Moon. This is literally the most exciting startup you will ever work at in your life, and possibly, in our generation. This is a massive responsibility. Life is short, and we are all going to pass one day. Do you want to live an average life, working as a cog in the machine, or do you want to be remembered in the stars? If you believe you will do your life's work by joining our mission at GRU, email me directly at skyler@gru.space with 3 bullet points: 1. What avionics or boards have you built that has flown into space/landed on the Moon/landed on Mars? Evidence of flight heritage 2. What are you world class at? 3. Why is being part of shaping human history via building the first bricks on the Moon, cities on the Moon and Mars, ultimately impacting trillions of future unborn lives that will walk the roads and infrastructure we build at GRU is too existential to not work on? Application Link: https://lnkd.in/g7f-GfpK

NASA's 2026 reauthorization is moving forward with broad bipartisan and industry support. I will link the bill text in the comments. If we stay on the Moon, we have to decide how to power it. Solar gets you part of the way. Nuclear does the rest. The bill pushes NASA to buy commercial lunar power, with nuclear squarely in scope, and sets a timeline that puts demos in this decade. That helps solve the chicken-and-egg problem where nobody buys until it is proven, and nothing is proven without a buyer. In parallel, companies like GRU Space (YC W26) are sketching serious lunar infrastructure concepts, including hospitality, as part of a future cislunar economy. Any long-lived power system that supports that kind of footprint will almost certainly sit inside a "safety zone" under the Artemis Accords. On paper, those zones are temporary and operational. In practice, a decades-long power site starts to look close to a de facto claim in a treaty system that still prohibits national appropriation. We are also getting more comfortable moving these systems around. In February, the US military flew a five-megawatt microreactor on C-17s as a logistics and safety test, unfueled in flight. If a reactor on a cargo jet feels routine, putting one on a rocket to a crater is not a huge leap. The reauthorization sits right at that intersection: nuclear as the practical answer to power, and safety zones as the legal fault line. How would you draw that line so future lunar power assets stay defensible under the Outer Space Treaty?

I booked a trip to the Moon. Well — I reserved the hotel stay, at least. Transportation is… still TBD (currently at $100k/kg it's $12M rt) This week, as an early birthday gift to myself, I booked a stay on the Moon for 2032 with GRU Space (YC W26) Six years early. Plenty of unknowns. Accommodation: confirmed. Ride there: very much a work in progress. On paper, this sounds a little unhinged. But here's where it starts to feel strangely obvious. I grew up in Houston, minutes from NASA Johnson Space Center. Space wasn’t sci-fi — it was neighbors, parents’ friends, and dinner-table conversation. My first job in high school was as a tour guide at Space Center Houston, explaining missions, failures, and firsts to visitors who saw it all as magic. For me, it was just… home. My first “real” job was interning at Lockheed Martin at JSC, working on a health rack for the International Space Station — life-support systems, human constraints, and what it actually takes to survive off Earth. Growing up, almost everyone’s parents worked in space. One neighbor’s dad was a food scientist who literally had a lab in his garage. My girl scout troop leader's husband trained almost every astronaut that went up in the shuttle program. He's one of the dads below. >50% of our community worked at JSC or a contractor of it. So I don’t think about the Moon as “space tourism.” Frontiers always open the same way: Science → Reliability → Infrastructure → Experience Tourism isn’t the reason we go first. It’s what shows up after the systems exist. The real value of frontiers isn’t the trip itself — it’s what comes out of it: - Autonomy - Robotics - Closed-loop life support - Energy systems - Human–machine coordination. Everest proved the human body. Antarctica helped us understand Earth. The Moon is about proving we’re a multi-environment species. So no — this wasn’t a bet on a launch date. It was a bet that humans will keep doing what we’ve always done: Commit early, build the hard systems, and turn impossibilities into infrastructure. For me, this doesn’t feel like a leap. It feels like a continuation. 🌕🚀 And if this resonates with you, hit up Skyler Chan and reserve your spot with me. Group trip 2032. let's go.

However, the imagination that one day people of all kinds go on holiday on the moon, is wild, exciting and truly connected to what Utopian Optimism is. 🚀🌑

A lunar hotel is now taking reservations, with stays starting at $10 million. And for a $1 million deposit you can secure your five-night stay. GRU Space (YC W26), a U.S. startup, plans to open the first hotel on the Moon in 2032. The initial habitat will be an inflatable structure transported from Earth, equipped with life support and radiation shielding, and designed to host four guests. Experiences will include lunar walks and low-gravity golf. Founder Skyler Chan states the hotel serves as an economic vessel to enable broader colonization. GRU’s roadmap begins with a 2029 demonstration mission to fabricate bricks from lunar regolith and test a modular habitat system. A subsequent mission will prepare a foundation within a lunar cave. The 2032 mission will deploy and open the hotel. Follow Bots n' Beans for daily robotics, humanoids, and tech insights. Follow Future of Built for robotics and automation in construction. Follow Annin Robotics to learn and start building your own AR4 robot.

Voxel Energy builds data centers coupled with onsite power generation, enabling data center commissioning in months, not years. $3 Trillion in data center projects are slated for construction by 2030, yet half may never be built due to power constraints. Voxel bypasses the gridlock throttling the American AI boom and unlocks billions in pull-in revenue. Voxel leverages the ever-expanding pool of second-life EV batteries to build energy storage capacity into the hundreds of megawatt-hours and beyond. These batteries, coupled with solar and Voxel's novel DC microgrid architecture, enable grid-scale power without the grid-scale delays. The founding team is composed of ex-Tesla engineers and data center construction managers with recognition for commercial EV manufacturing in the US. Voxel’s systems are live, operational, and already running off-grid compute. Batteries are being amassed to accommodate demand. And hundreds of acres are already under contract. Reservations are now open for existing sites. Capacity is limited. Reach out if you’re tired of waiting to power your compute. founders@voxelenergy.com Read the white paper: https://lnkd.in/gu_4xwHx Congrats on the launch Casey Spencer, Evan Schmidt, and Max Pfeiffer! https://lnkd.in/gia28Pb4

2026 has been a breakneck year for the space industry. Americans circled the Moon for the first time since 1972. SpaceX filed for a record-shattering $1.5 trillion IPO. The line between science fiction and reality seems to have dissolved as every week brings a fresh announcement of an orbital logistics or asteroid mining startup exiting stealth with a valuation that rivals legacy corporations. As big as this moment feels, I think we’re still in the early innings of where the space economy is headed. Launch costs have dropped by orders of magnitude and satellites that used to take years to build now take weeks. That means more hardware in orbit, more data coming back down, feeding applications in agriculture, insurance, defense, and climate that barely existed five years ago. And a whole new supply chain is forming around not just getting to space but staying there: manufacturing, mining, energy, compute, communications. We've mapped the next generation of U.S. space tech startups into fifteen categories across three layers of the value chain: 🚀 GOING UP 𝗟𝗮𝘂𝗻𝗰𝗵 𝗩𝗲𝗵𝗶𝗰𝗹𝗲𝘀: Rockets getting payloads to orbit 𝗢𝗧𝗩𝘀 & 𝗣𝗿𝗼𝗽𝘂𝗹𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻: In-space transportation and the engines that power it 𝗚𝗿𝗼𝘂𝗻𝗱 𝗦𝘆𝘀𝘁𝗲𝗺𝘀 & 𝗢𝗽𝘀: Mission control, space traffic management, infrastructure 𝗦𝗮𝘁𝗲𝗹𝗹𝗶𝘁𝗲 𝗕𝘂𝗶𝗹𝗱: Faster, cheaper satellite design and build 𝗠𝗮𝗻𝘂𝗳𝗮𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴, 𝗦𝗼𝗳𝘁𝘄𝗮𝗿𝗲, 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗙𝗶𝗻𝗮𝗻𝗰𝗲: Precision manufacturing, simulation, mission tools, financing to make it happen 🛰️ IN SPACE 𝗦𝘁𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀 & 𝗛𝗮𝗯𝗶𝘁𝗮𝘁𝘀: Next-generation crewed platforms 𝗦𝗲𝗿𝘃𝗶𝗰𝗲𝘀 & 𝗗𝗲𝗳𝗲𝗻𝘀𝗲: Space domain awareness, satellite servicing, orbital security 𝗠𝗮𝗻𝘂𝗳𝗮𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴, 𝗠𝗶𝗻𝗶𝗻𝗴 & 𝗟𝘂𝗻𝗮𝗿: In-space production, asteroid mining, lunar surface operations 𝗘𝗻𝗲𝗿𝗴𝘆 & 𝗖𝗼𝗺𝗽𝘂𝘁𝗲: Power generation, orbital data centers 𝗧𝗲𝗹𝗲𝗰𝗼𝗺 𝗖𝗼𝗻𝘀𝘁𝗲𝗹𝗹𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀: Satellite networks delivering connectivity 🌎 COMING DOWN 𝗘𝗮𝗿𝘁𝗵 𝗢𝗯𝘀𝗲𝗿𝘃𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻: Imaging and sensing platforms monitoring the planet from above 𝗖𝗼𝗻𝗻𝗲𝗰𝘁𝗶𝘃𝗶𝘁𝘆 & 𝗜𝗼𝗧: Satellite-powered networks reaching where terrestrial infrastructure can't 𝗔𝗻𝗮𝗹𝘆𝘁𝗶𝗰𝘀 & 𝗣𝗹𝗮𝘁𝗳𝗼𝗿𝗺𝘀: Turning space-derived data into actionable intelligence for enterprise, government 𝗣𝗡𝗧 & 𝗡𝗮𝘃𝗶𝗴𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻: Next-generation positioning, timing systems beyond GPS 𝗦𝗗𝗔 & 𝗦𝘁𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗼𝘀𝗽𝗵𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗰: Tracking objects in orbit, high-altitude platforms for persistent Earth intelligence Top VCs from Andreessen Horowitz, Founders Fund, Khosla Ventures, Sequoia Capital, Accel, Bessemer Venture Partners, BlackRock, Y Combinator, Craft Ventures, and Venrock are investing heavily in this sector. Here at Commonweal Ventures, we are actively seeking founders building in space tech. If you're also building in this space (see what I did there?), get in touch. And if we missed a startup in our map, let us know.

I am super grateful to Anurag Bonthalapati for chatting with me right after the Y Combinator batch about the history and vision for Beyond Reach Labs (YC W26). First, Anurag Bonthalapati is an incredible mechanisms engineer working in Space deployables for over a decade now, so we could have chatted for hours, but he is also an incredible interviewer and kept me on track. We talk about the work with NASA - National Aeronautics and Space Administration during my PhD with my collaborators and co-inventors Zac Manchester, Sawyer Thomas, PhD, and Jeff Lipton (of course I have a bag of prototypes to show off). We talk about how timing and a bit of fate (maybe self-fulfilling) brought Pele Collins and I together working again, the first time since our time at Penn. I get to put my foot in my mouth for my overly ambitious plan for our first launch 😩. We talk about why "YC is the YC for Space," shoutout to Garry Tan And we talk about why now is the most exciting time for new space companies.

Launch mass to orbit has improved a lot. But we are still volume-limited. That matters because the next generation of space infrastructure will not fit neatly inside a fairing. We need structures that can stow into a tight package, survive launch, deploy reliably on orbit, and then become stiff enough to actually be useful. That is why I was so excited to come across what Mitchell Fogelson, PhD and Pele Collins are building at Beyond Reach Labs (YC W26). Beyond Reach is building large 3D deployable structures that pack into a very small volume, then deploy into rigid, high-stiffness structures in space. I have spent 9 years in this industry, specifically working on space deployables, and these guys are THE real deal. This is one of the technologies that has to work if we want large-scale space infrastructure to become real, from antennas and habitats to in-space manufacturing and eventually data centers in orbit. Anisha J. and I recorded a full episode with Mitch on The Just Rocket Science Show. 🎙️ Check it out here: https://lnkd.in/diaWM6-g
Beyond Reach Labs is hiring the best interns this summer! Apply now to work with Pele Collins and Mitchell Fogelson, PhD: https://lnkd.in/ezFFuiiK

Graeme Harrison joined Nihal Kurth on Deeptech Decoded this morning. Another KILLER episode. VC, startups, conviction, future works, and why firms that move fast and think independently are going to dominate the next decade. They got a chance to discuss the why and how behind augur vc investing in Beyond Reach Labs (YC W26), Cumulus Labs (YC W26) and Aravolta. TL;DR: we invested because they are winners. augur vc and Simply Silicon are being built around a very different worldview of venture and technology. Faster decisions. Higher conviction. Real technical depth. Less institutionalized thinking. More willingness to back people and ideas before consensus forms around them. A lot is happening right now. The people paying attention can already feel the shift underway. Dan Chapman, Oma Wilkie

"I took two seconds of talking to them to be like, yup, this is it." This is Graeme Harrison on backing Beyond Reach Labs (YC W26). Before the demo day. He writes the check the same day. He still picks up when things go wrong. And in the meanwhile, he is building Simply Silicon. Graeme backed Mikey Brown when he was 18, Pele Collins and Mitchell Fogelson, PhD in two seconds. In between the love letters to Mikey, we also managed to cover ✓ Red flags in deep tech investors ✓ His hands on perspective as a builder and backer in AI infrastructure ✓ The Beyond Reach Labs investment: from first contact to wire transfer ✓ The VC behaviors that make great founders walk out of VC meetings thinking they are the problem. Full episode in comments.

500+ founders. 1,500+ nominations. 100 companies. The Black Flag 100 is back 🏴‍☠️ The premise is simple. Founders know who's building the next great companies, so we asked them one question: "Which early-stage companies are you most excited about right now?" (Only rules: Out of stealth in the last two years, not on last year's list) We tally up the 100 most-nominated companies and the BF100 for 2026 is ready to set sail. The list also celebrates the great investors backing these companies. Here's what they're building: 🛡️ 27% Defense 🚀 22% Aerospace 🤖 22% Robotics & Manufacturing 🧠 19% AI And where: 🌉 40% Bay Area 🏖️ 20% SoCal 🗽 18% East Coast 🤠 8% Texas Hard tech. Hard problems. The next generation of critical technology, being built mostly on the coasts and increasingly everywhere. To every founder on the list, and every founder still out there chasing storms over certainty: keep building. We need you. It's a pirate's life 🏴‍☠️ Full list in the comments.

I defended my thesis over 11 months ago, and a lot has changed since then... But it is important when you have moments to celebrate that you take the time to celebrate them, so I decided to walk at graduation 11 months later. There are many people to thank for this day, starting with my family, Thanks to my advisors Jonathan Cagan, Ph.D., P.E. and Zac Manchester, Thanks to my fiancè Nanami Mano, PT, DPT, CSRS, Thanks to my peers Dr. Mary Anna Ebbert, Dr. Kara Ferner, Dr. Sarah Costrell, Ph.D. and many not pictured, Thanks Carnegie Mellon University Big thanks to Pele Collins I am so excited to be able to continue this work with you building Beyond Reach Labs (YC W26) together. Alright, enough celebrating, back to work 😛

Coming up next at Deeptech Decoded LIVE: Graeme Harrison. We recently hosted Pele Collins of Beyond Reach Labs (YC W26) and now it's time to turn the table and face one of their early backers. Very few investors actually understand AI infrastructure. And even fewer build it while backing frontier founders at the same time. Graeme does both. Building at Simply Silicon and investing at augur vc. There is nothing more enjoyable than reasoning with opinionated people. Bring your hardest questions for the hardest hardware problems you are tackling. 🗓️ RSVP in comments.

Engineered for the world, built in America. Precision Neuroscopics - Developing medical technologies focused on EEG to gain insight into worsening brain conditions and improve patient experience. D'ANGELO TECHNOLOGIES, LLC - Engineering R&D and technical solutions spanning management, research, prototype development, manufacturing, and testing. HAMR Industries LLC - Advanced materials and manufacturing processes engineered for components operating in harsh and extreme environments. Beyond Reach Labs (YC W26) - A patent-protected modification to deep learning systems that reduces error and network size using neuroscience-based principles. These picks belong to Pittsburgh. You nominated them. You voted for them. And today we celebrate them. Congratulations to every company in the Second Round! The mission is to back founders who view the world through unique lenses, with ambitions to build the digital and physical infrastructure of the future - like Aron and Kyle at Objection, Mitchell and Pele at Beyond Reach Labs (YC W26), Aleksander at Stendr, and Benjamin, Drew and Suvrit at Octet. Thank you to my fantastic LPs: Edition Ventures, Apeiron Investment Group, Christian Angermayer, Nick Nigam, Wilson Sonsini Goodrich & Rosati, Seth Helfgott, good friends and former colleagues from Thiel Capital, Altair Group, Farvatn, Ragnar Horn, Nils Alexander Horn, Sayonara as, Emil Braathen, Aleksander Leonard Larsen, Karl Rosander, Daniel Langkilde, Revalence Ventures, Jonathan Sibilia - and the good people who prefer to remain in the shadows. Time to go Off Piste.

On probably one of Pittsburgh's busiest weeks with the NFL Draft this weekend, Mayor Corey O'Connor reached out to me to congratulate Beyond Reach Labs (YC W26) on our early success...don't worry I was able to answer the phone before it sent him to voice mail (thanks Apple). Pittsburgh has been critical to my success from programs at Project Olympus CMU, Swartz Center for Entrepreneurship, InnovatePGH, Innovation Works, Keystone Space Collaborative and Robotics Factory. Pittsburgh provided us with the pre-seed funding through McGinnis Venture Competition, Keystone Space Competition, and Invent Penn State Competition. I have to thank so many people from the Pittsburgh community for supporting us, Meredith Meyer Grelli, Melanie Simko, Dave Mawhinney, Cara Giannandrea, Scott M, Kevin Dowling, Sara Longo, Milán Minott, Zoë Karabinus, Namrata Banerjee, Andrew Katon, Max Polec, PCC, Charles Mansfield III, and I know so many more... At Beyond Reach Labs (YC W26) we are very proud of our roots and very excited for people to catch up to how amazing Pittsburgh entrepreneurship is!

This is what YC calls “pre-seed” in 2026. 196 companies presented at YC W26 Demo Day. 14 crossed $1M ARR before presenting. 14% - avg. weekly revenue growth across the batch. Fastest ever recorded. I went through all of them. Here are 10 that stopped me — and why: 🌾 GrazeMate (YC W26) The only agtech in the entire batch. No comments here. I’m clearly biased 😁 Founder grew up on a 6,000-head cattle station. Now builds AI drones to herd them. Founder-market fit on steroids. 🚀 Beyond Reach Labs (YC W26) Solar arrays the size of a football field — in orbit. $325M in LOIs. Not a pitch. A pipeline. 🤖 Asimov (YC W26) They collect human movement data to train humanoid robots. This is not robotics. This is “we are preparing data for the robot economy”. 🏥 Beacon Health (YC W26) Backed by Accel and Sequoia Capital scout before Demo Day ended. Co-founded by a Stanford/Harvard physician. Largest healthcare raise in the batch. 🔬 Synthetic Sciences (YC W26) “Claude Code for science”. $1.4M raised before YC even started. 📦 Pocket (YC W26) AI hardware. 30,000 devices shipped in 5 months. 50% MoM growth. YC company with Series B traction. 🔐 Hex Security (YC W26) $1M ARR in 8 weeks. I had to reread that twice. 🧠 ARC Prize Foundation A nonprofit in YC. They build AGI benchmarks used by OpenAI, Anthropic, Google. Yes. A nonprofit. 💪 Captain (YC W26) Garry Tan’s personal pick. When the YC CEO personally points — you look. 🎮 Cardboard Highest-upvoted Hacker News launch in the batch. Community signal before investor signal. This batch feels like: “Okay. Fun is over. Let’s rebuild the real world.” If you had to bet on one — which one?

So excited to have Founders Capital and Jeremy Hindle's support for Beyond Reach Labs (YC W26)! We love bringing on former operators and former Y Combinator founders as they bring valuable insight and connections.
Thanks so much for the highlight!

Beyond Reach Labs (YC W26). Pele Collins and Mitchell Fogelson, PhD are about to define a new category. // Update: Pele is the CTO and Mitchell is the CEO. That's what happens if you aim to publish while traveling. If you believe orbital data centers and power-hungry spacecraft are inevitable, you are implicitly betting on them, too. They are building a new class of deployable orbital power and thermal infrastructure. Arrays that go from dining-table to football-field scale from a single launch. The bottleneck for space is increasingly shifting from "can we get up there?" to "can we power it?" Three hard constraints: 1/ Rockets are volume-constrained. 2/ Once it is up there, it has to work. Perfectly. In one of the most unforgiving environments humans have ever reached. 3/ More power in space always demands more surface area. Two engineers building the power backbone for space infrastructure. Support them if you want to see them win. ~ 📌 Deeptech Decoded → LIVE Podcast + AMA RSVP in comments. April 8th, 2026.
Congrats to Starcloud and our friends / mentors Philip Johnston and Ezra Feilden on this incredible achievement. Compute in space unlocks so many opportunities and value! So excited for this team.

TechCrunch coverage for Beyond Reach Labs (YC W26) — VERY honoured to invest! Mitchell Fogelson, PhD and Pele Collins are changing the way the energy business works. They are one of the strongest teams in the world today. It is inevitable that they will win the big prize in space energy supply. The scale space offers will dwarf the terrestrial energy business within a few years. It will result in massive wealth creation, the likes of which the average energy CEO has never seen or imagined before. We are in a revolutionary period in human history, and energy is at the tip of the spear. Augur Energy AI Funds I & II are pure play against this moment in time. augur vc Dan Chapman Oma Wilkie Michael Brown

EMF Space, Inc. has selected Beyond Reach Labs (YC W26) as its preferred supplier for large-scale deployable solar array structures. EMF Space, Inc. is building pivotal Space-Based Solar Power solutions to solve critical power bottlenecks on Earth, and Beyond Reach Lab's technology is a key enabler of the scale of on-orbit power generation required to make that vision a reality.
Mitch is excellent, and I’m very excited to work with Beyond Reach on this!

DEAL ALERT: we have invested in two new companies, Beyond Reach Labs (YC W26) and Lunetronic, two key enablers of orbital AI infra. First up: Beyond Reach builds gigantic deployable solar arrays designed to deliver many times the power per launch of existing systems. This solves the biggest bottleneck in space today -- scaling up power. Next up is Lunetronic. This company offers an Infiniband-speed inter-satellite communication capability, unlocking a true high capacity orbital backhaul network for the first time. The promise of AI infra on orbit can only be fully realized by climbing further up the tech tree. We are underwriting the best teams in the world enabling the convergence of the AI and Orbital infra asset classes -- this is what excellence looks like. These two companies are definite category makers, and we are honored they let us allocate. No other venture platform in the world has indexed the AI infrastructure value chain as tightly and thoroughly as augur vc.

Beyond Reach Labs (YC W26) and Lunetronic are going to the moon and beyond!! Not everyday you get to say that literally and figuratively. :-) Shout out to Pele Collins and Mitchell Fogelson, PhD who are crushing their build and raising right now out of the YC W26 cohort.
Back in PGH this week building with Pele Collins at Robotics Factory. Such an incredible resource for deeptech companies in the Pittsburgh Area. Thanks for the support Innovation Works.

Met these guys a couple weeks ago, and recently sat down with Mitchell in SF. If you believe that everything in the space industry is going to get bigger & better, I recommend reaching out to them! Awesome stuff Mitchell Fogelson, PhD Pele Collins! 🚀 Congrats!

We love watching our Pittsburgh Startups get the recognition (and support) they deserve. OctaPulse (YC W26) and Beyond Reach Labs (YC W26) have just graduated from YCombinator! Beyond Reach Labs (YC W26), founded by CMU PhD Mitchell Fogelson, PhD and SpaceX veteran Pele Collins, was accepted into Y Combinator's Winter 2026 cohort. The company is building deployable solar arrays that launch the size of a dining table and expand to the size of a football field in orbit — addressing a projected 10,000x surge in orbital power demand by 2035. They have already secured letters of intent from space companies totaling over $175M and are targeting their first in-space demonstration flight for 2027. OctaPulse (YC W26), a fellow Y Combinator Winter 2026 company, is also a Pittsburgh and CMU startup to celebrate. Co-founded by Paul L. Grech and Rohan Singh, OctaPulse uses AI vision and robotics to automate quality inspection at fish hatcheries, starting with broodstock phenotyping and juvenile deformity inspection — two of the most critical steps in fish production that are still done entirely by hand. They are already deployed with Riverence, North America's largest trout producer, on a six-figure annual contract. The broader goal is to bring automation across the entire fish production lifecycle for the $300 billion aquaculture industry and they are building it right here in Pittsburgh. While building and pushing their companies forward, they've moved through programs like our Anvil Founder Coaching program and Pathways Robotics Fellowship, along with finding support along the way the from other ecosystem partners such as Innovation Works, the Carnegie Mellon University Swartz Center for Entrepreneurship, and more. Congratulations to both of these teams, we can't wait to see what's next! Looking for support on your founder journey? Innovatepgh.com

Why going to Space is like packing for a road trip... Rockets are massive structures, but only a fraction of that volume is usable for payloads. Even as we dream of vehicles like Starship, New Glenn, and others with larger fairings coming online, one constraint remains constant: Launch volume is finite! And some of the hardest systems to pack are the ones we need most: Solar arrays Thermal radiators Large antennas Large aperture imaging Structural booms These systems fundamentally depend on size to perform. So we face some key decisions: → Launch multiple times (cost, coordination, schedule risk) → Get much better at packing and deploying large systems I think we will need both in the long run. That’s the problem space we’re excited to be working on at Beyond Reach Labs (YC W26), building structures that launch compact and deploy reliably at scale once in orbit.

What a post to wake up to from Philip Johnston, the man who has been preaching (against a wave) about large-scale computing in Space long before SpaceX and others jumped on board. Thank you for the vote of confidence, and excited about the future you and the Starcloud team are working to bring about. It's an honor for Mitchell Fogelson, PhD and I to follow in the footsteps of Ezra Feilden, Adi Oltean and you through our own Y Combinator journey now with Beyond Reach Labs (YC W26), and hopefully we'll be working together soon on the next generation of Space megastructures (assuming your good track record stays intact)!

With four brave explorers moonward bound and solar array deployment happening on Integrity right now, I’m beyond excited to share that today Quorum Capital Partners made its first space tech investment in Beyond Reach Labs (YC W26) 🚀 Mitchell Fogelson, PhD has been a dear friend for over a decade. And, it’s been a thrill following his journey: from visiting him in Tokyo where he worked on robotics R&D at Rakuten, to watching his dissertation defense on the linkage-based systems that are now the backbone of his startup. Here’s to the decade ahead, where Mitch and Pele Collins build the backbone of space infrastructure. Here’s to making what’s beyond reach today, possible tomorrow!

Thanks Diana Hu and Vivian Midha Shen for being our increible partners. More exciting news to share soon…

A lot of “venture capital” out of Canada is more about virtue signalling than making money. People want to be seen standing near the right themes, the right industries, the right slogans. They want to show the government just how obedient they are, in hopes of scoring another ride on the taxpayer-funded management fee gravy train. This causes deal quality to fall by the wayside in favor of policy alignment. The same effect in this and other sectors is why so many Canadian darlings fall apart. There is nothing there. Climate in Canada was especially prone to this. Fortunately for our LPs at augur vc, we are completely independent of any government influence, and we invest for the purpose of making massive returns, NOT being culture warriors. In my opinion this is more, not less, patriotic. We generate actual wealth for Canadian investors, not just feel-good headlines. One of our biggest wins to date is our recent investment in Beyond Reach Labs (YC W26), who we had the chance to meet in person this past week at YC Demo Day. By moving incredibly fast on our belief that scaled-up solar is a category play for Space, the little Canadian VC that could managed to write the FIRST cheque into what turned out to be a global consensus winner. This means Canadian investors now have exposure to one of the most important teams in Space today. A big part of Venture-land in Canada, especially the parts dedicated to begging for taxpayer dollars, hates the idea that Augur can exist without government backing. We are constantly getting excluded from industry dialogue here in our home country, despite having one of the strongest track records and growth rates of any capital platform. It doesn’t matter though, because we are blowing them all out of the water when it comes to winning allocations and creating wealth for our investors. That is why Canadian LPs, who are notoriously anti-VC, keep calling me about how they can get into our funds. The underdog always wins in the end.

Beyond Reach Labs (YC W26) builds deployable solar arrays and radiators that launch compact and expand to football-field scale in orbit. Compute in space is growing fast, but rockets are volume-constrained! The systems that enable next-generation spacecraft — solar panels for power and radiators for heat rejection — only work well when they’re large. As satellites, space stations, and in-space data centers demand more power, they need more surface area. But today’s deployable structures don’t scale well: they take up too much room when stowed, they're expensive, and they often end up too flexible once deployed. Power generation and heat dissipation are becoming the bottlenecks for the next generation of space infrastructure. Beyond Reach Labs designs and manufactures patented deployable structures that pack tightly for launch and expand into extremely large, stiff arrays in orbit. Their technology improves stowage efficiency and structural performance, enabling 100kW+ systems needed for orbital data centers and other large space infrastructure. The team combines deep flight experience and advanced deployable structures research. Pele Collins spent seven years at SpaceX leading parachute engineering and production across 30+ Dragon missions, and Dr. Mitchell Fogelson earned his PhD at Carnegie Mellon, focused on large deployable space structures with NASA. They’ve been building together since 2013 as freshmen in college and are focused on making large space structures reliable, scalable, and practical! Congrats on the launch, Pele Collins and Mitchell Fogelson, PhD! https://lnkd.in/g4SBNA6i

We just got funded $500K by Y Combinator!! This post used to say something else. But we pivoted to: Traverse

We got accepted into Y Combinator at 18. Two months ago I was in a Waterloo dorm room with a broken heater, trying to decide if I should drop out after one semester. If I've learned anything so far, it's that most of the good things in my life came from doing things rather than deliberating over them. There's a version of me that played it safer, and I'm sure he wouldn't be in SF typing this right now. Thanks everyone for the support!

Traverse is a data research lab building the training data that makes frontier models superhuman in ambiguous, judgment-heavy domains. Reinforcement learning has pushed models beyond human performance in math and coding as these fields have clean, verifiable feedback. But most economically valuable work, like law, sales, system design, creative judgment, does not. Current incumbents use synthetic data to create environments; experts at laptops answering questions, creating rubrics, annotating. That’s great at making models average, but it will not make them superhuman. Traverse is defining a new type of data category. Find out more: founders@traverse.so Congrats on the launch, Lance Yan and Zachary Yu! https://lnkd.in/gaZgQkCt
And.... we're live. We give AI labs the training data that enables their frontier models to acquire taste. To find out more: https://traverse.so (We're also hiring!!)

Today, we are announcing Traverse. (https://traverse.so) We are building reinforcement learning environments for long-horizon agent journeys, our goal is to automate all economically valuable work. AI systems have made rapid progress on tasks that are easy to describe and easy to evaluate, yet they continue to struggle with the work that defines most professions. We believe this gap exists because agents are trained in environments that optimize for convenience rather than realism, and because the industry has largely converged on data collection methods that fail to capture how work is actually done. The bottleneck to agent capability is no longer architecture or compute but data quality and environment fidelity. Much of today's training data is contrived and produced by people who do not deeply understand the domains being modeled, which optimizes for volume and surface correctness but strips out the reasoning and judgment and context that define real expertise. High-quality training signal comes from observation, not fabrication. The most valuable data is generated when experts perform real work in real environments, where decisions are shaped by experience and tradeoffs and failure. We are starting with software engineering as it is the most verifiable and deterministic domain that exists today. Tests pass or fail and builds succeed or break. Software also creates a natural flywheel where agents that write code can eventually improve the infrastructure used to train them, which is a feedback loop we want to establish as early as possible. Our approach is to assemble small teams of world-class domain experts and world-class ML engineers, and to hold that bar fixed as we expand into law, medicine, finance, and beyond. The path to general intelligence is to traverse through every domain where work happens. https://traverse.so

about.Overview:
The Moon Hotel Company.

GRU builds durable space habitats so humans can live on the Moon and Mars.
Our first missions will mine lunar regolith to construct a permanent habitat on the moon for commercial space tourism — a hotel on the moon.

We don’t stop at Moon hotels. GRU’s long-term plan:

1. Build the first hotel on the Moon. GRU solves off‑world surface habitation.
2. Build America’s first Moon base: roads, mass drivers, warehouses, and physical infrastructure.
3. Repeat on Mars and build the first cities there.
4. Own property on the Moon and Mars as these economies grow.
5. Reinvest profits into resource utilization systems on the Moon, Mars, asteroids, and beyond—reaching our final form: Galactic Resource Utilization.

Beyond Reach Labs, Inc. is an innovative startup developing cutting-edge metamaterials and deployable technologies for challenging environments. With a vision to revolutionize industries crippled by volume or labor limitations -- such as medical, defense, and space -- Beyond Reach Labs is dedicated to creating robust, deployable solutions that address critical needs in remote and demanding locations. Committed to pushing the boundaries of technology, Beyond Reach Labs combines advanced research with practical engineering to deliver impactful, real-world solutions.
